text: Hi everyone, this is Dr. Mario Leyba. Whenever doing an ETEP from a lateral approach, sometimes it can be difficult to identify the contralateral posterior sheath during the crossover. In this short video, I'm going to show you how I use ICG to help identify the correct plane. This is a fairly small hernia. However, a TAR that I recently did, the anatomy can be quite disrupted and any help that you can get to identify this plane can be useful.

The surgeon dissects the medial ipsilateral posterior sheath and angles the scissors towards the linea alba to locate the crossover point. The dissection progresses slowly to identify the contralateral posterior sheath, with ICG used to assist in plane identification.

The surgeon gently dissects across from the ipsilateral to the contralateral side, closely following the linea alba. A hint of green fluorescence from the ICG dye becomes visible, indicating the interface of the contralateral posterior sheath.

The surgeon gently dissects the hernia sac on the ipsilateral side, using a decompressing quick peak port in the left upper quadrant for potential decompression. The dissection progresses to the contralateral side, where the ICG clearly delineates the medial side of the contralateral posterior sheath.

The surgeon begins dissection approximately one centimeter lateral to the interface, gently pushing off infused tissue on the contralateral side marked by ICG. The space for the ETEP procedure has been previously created and is accessed from the ipsilateral side.
The video shows the interface between the ICG-infused tissue and normal tissue on the right side of the retro-rectus space.
